questions.question: What does CAPWAP stand for?
options: Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points
Cisco Access Point Wireless Access Protocol
Centralized Access Point Wireless Access Protocol
Control Access Provisioning Wireless Architecture Protocol
questions.explanation: CAPWAP stands for Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points — the protocol between lightweight APs and WLC.

questions.question: Which UDP port does CAPWAP use for its control tunnel (encrypted)?
questions.explanation: CAPWAP control tunnel uses UDP 5246 (encrypted); data tunnel uses UDP 5247.

questions.question: What is an autonomous AP?
options: Standalone AP; self-contained, each configured individually; connects via trunk
AP that relies on a WLC and tunnels traffic via CAPWAP
AP managed via a cloud dashboard
questions.explanation: An autonomous AP is standalone; self-contained, each configured individually; connects via trunk to carry multiple VLANs.

questions.question: What is split-MAC architecture?
options: Division of 802.11 MAC-layer functions between lightweight AP (real-time) and WLC (management/control)
Running two MAC address tables on a single switch
A security technique that splits MAC addresses across VLANs
Separating wireless and wired MAC tables
questions.explanation: Split-MAC architecture is the division of 802.11 MAC-layer functions between lightweight AP (real-time) and WLC (management/control).

questions.question: Which lightweight AP mode is the default and offers one or more BSSs tunneling traffic to the WLC?
questions.explanation: Local mode is the default lightweight AP mode; offers one or more BSSs and tunnels traffic to the WLC.

questions.question: What AP mode lets the AP locally switch traffic if the CAPWAP tunnel goes down?
questions.explanation: FlexConnect mode is a lightweight AP mode that lets the AP locally switch traffic if the CAPWAP tunnel to the WLC goes down.

questions.question: What does SSID stand for?
questions.explanation: SSID stands for Service Set Identifier — the wireless network name.

questions.question: Which WLC type supports up to ~6000 APs and is suited to large enterprise campus?
questions.explanation: Unified WLC is a hardware-appliance WLC in a central location; supports up to ~6000 APs; suited to large enterprise campus.

questions.question: What AP mode listens on the wired side (ARP) and correlates with the WLC's rogue list?
questions.explanation: Rogue detector mode: AP does not use its radio; listens on the wired side (ARP traffic) and correlates with WLC's rogue list.

questions.question: What is a Beacon frame?
options: 802.11 management frame periodically broadcast by an AP to advertise the BSS
A probe request from a client
A data frame carrying user traffic
A control frame used for RTS/CTS
questions.explanation: Beacon is an 802.11 management frame periodically broadcast by an AP to advertise the BSS (used by passive scanning).

questions.question: Which category of 802.11 frames includes RTS, CTS, and ACK?
questions.explanation: 802.11 control frames include RTS, CTS, and ACK. Management frames: beacons, probes, authentication, association.

questions.question: What is Cisco Mobility Express?
options: WLC functionality embedded in an AP itself; supports ~100 APs; suited to branch offices
A cloud-based WLC running in a data center
A hardware appliance WLC for large campuses
questions.explanation: Cisco Mobility Express is WLC functionality embedded in an AP itself; supports ~100 APs; suited to branch offices.
